transcript: We're starting with breaking news about Bitcoin. In the last hour, the value of the cryptocurrency has hit an all-time high. A single coin has achieved $69,202 in trading today, breaking its previous record of exactly one cent short of $69,000. That record was set back in November 2021. Well, since then, Bitcoin has gone on quite a roller coaster. And of course, the world of cryptocurrencies in general has been embroiled in controversy over failed trading platforms. Let's bring in Michelle Fleury, who's our North America business correspondent, and who can explain to us what is behind this recent surge in Bitcoin. Yeah, I mean, after 18 months in which we've seen bankruptcies, we've seen court cases that have really led people to actually question the foundations of this whole entire ecosystem. Suddenly, here we are again talking about Bitcoin tag hitting new record highs. And it's the continuation of a rally that's been sort of building now for a couple of months. I think there's a couple of factors behind it. One is the mainstreaming, if you like, of Bitcoin. You may recall earlier this year, we had regulators here in the United States open investing opportunities attached to cryptocurrencies to more investors. And since then, funds have been pouring into that. I think there was a report that as much as sort of, you know, huge figures have been raised. So that has kind of definitely increased things on the demand side. On the supply side, there is this process called the halving. It's part of the Bitcoin protocol that only a certain number of Bitcoin are ever issued. And periodically, once every four years, the kind of reward given to Bitcoin miners for the number of Bitcoins mined actually decreases. And there is speculation that the next halving will occur in May 2024, about four years to the date of the last one. So that may also have an impact on what investors think the supply will be. And all of this, I think you can sort of safely say, is leading to that sort of craze for laser eyes, which is the meme that cryptocurrency fans use when they're feeling optimistic about Bitcoin. Yeah, we're going to park Bitcoin for a moment. We will be coming back to it with a guest in a moment, though. But Michelle, some other breaking news you're looking at. A global outage of Facebook and Instagram. Productivity must be soaring. What more can you tell us? So users of Meta's sort of suite of products, as you say, Facebook and Instagram, have reported problems logging in. Now, we see on X, the company has posted that they know there is an issue. But obviously, an outage like this is going to affect, you know, thousands of users. And we're trying to wait to find out more information, which we'll Thank you for watching. The company has posted that they know there is an issue, but obviously an outage like this is going to affect, you know, thousands of users. And we're trying to wait to find out more information, which we'll bring to you, of course, as we get it. No distractions. Michelle Fleury, thank you very much for that. Well, let's go back to Bitcoin. It's hitting that new record all time high. Let's bring in David Gerrard, author of the book Attack of the 50-Foot Blockchain, Scams, Drugs, Crooks and Cryptography. Based on that title, David, I think it's fair to say you're not a fan of Bitcoin or cryptocurrencies in general. What do you make of today's developments? So it's been really interesting. There's a real lack of retail dollars coming in, ordinary mum and dad dollars. They're the engine of any economic bubble, naive investors. what we've seen is a lot of odd stuff happening in the bitcoin market because this is a completely unregulated market by the way the um price discovery doesn't happen in the u.s it happens in offshore casinos such as binance, which is literally a criminally convicted conspiracy. They're currently under a $4 billion fine and severe restrictions. So if you have a feeling the crypto industry is a bit of a dodgy gamble, that would be a correct feeling because it really is a very minor thing in terms of economics um there's a lot of odd things happening with the price when you see the price jump three thousand dollars in the middle of the night 2 a.m new york time that won't be etfs which trade during stock market hours um there's a lot of questionable stable coins which may or may not be fully backed being issued and pumped into the markets okay so and also all the exchanges last week shut down withdrawals simultaneously including large ones like it's fair to say it's fair to say david you you smell a rat and you're suspicious um but you talk about investors is a quilted of red flags. Fair enough. You talk about investors being naive. I'm sure there's a lot of investors out there who feel pretty clever at the gains that they've made. Money is pouring into these ETFs at a rate of around $2 billion a week. And it does stand to reason that opening up this new manner of trading, these exchange traded funds, will trigger a surge in prices. Because you just open yourself up to a whole new swathe of investors who now see it as a safer investment, notwithstanding what others say about Bitcoin, about that has no intrinsic value. But opening up to exchange traded funds does kind of give it a badge of respectability. It does, but it's not clear that fresh dollars are actually coming in. It seems to be dollars moving from other parts of the ecosystem. For example, sell-offs of the Bitcoin miners or other companies that are tangential to Bitcoin moving that money into ETFs. We are absolutely not seeing seeing fresh dollars coming in so that's not a thing that we're seeing happen at all the retail trade in bitcoin is it's surprisingly dead for such large headline grabbing numbers there are others out there david who share your views about bitcoin i mean the us regulators the sec had to be dragged kicking and screaming into actually approving these etfs legal action forced that move um do you think regulators central banks should be shouting even louder about the risks they see uh with crypto in general ultimately if you're selling people you can get rich for free that's very attractive product, whether it's got anything to it or not. So at a certain point, all the regulators can do is say, look, this is trash, take care. They can stop promoters because you can stop people from promoting things that are a missold investment. But ultimately, if I want to set my money on fire i can probably do so we can all do that david gerrard's um author cryptic or critic of crypto thank you very much for that
